title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Volumes and Prices Declined Amidst Uncertainty Regarding Implementation of CARB Amendments
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(03rd March â€“ 09th March)
  • A total of 177,849 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 84.20% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a fall of 35.20% WoW as 1,098,904 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price decreased this week to $57.54/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording an 18.29%Â fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 2.25% in price, reaching $68.56/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price decreased by 5.24% WoW; on 11th March, the spot price stood at $57.75/MT and the benchmark price stood at $63.00/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices decreased by 0.80% WoW, D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices decreased by 5.19% WoW each.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The OALâ€™s disapproval of LCFS amendments on February 18, 2025, due to procedural concerns, led to a sharp drop in credit prices and trading volumes. CARB clarified the rejection was technical, not policy-related, and plans to revise and resubmit the amendments after public input. Meanwhile, Clean Fuels Alliance America voiced frustration over delays in the Renewable Fuel Standard program as the EPA finalized a rule postponing the 2024 RFS compliance deadline. At the same time, bipartisan lawmakers introduced the Renewable Fuel for Ocean-Going Vessels Act, backed by agriculture and maritime groups. The legislation classifies renewable fuel used in ocean-going vessels as â€œadditional renewable fuelâ€ under the Renewable Fuel Standard, allowing companies to retain Renewable Identification Number (RIN) credits when such fuel is used in eligible maritime vessels.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_593608" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 18.29% WoW fall, decreasing from $70.42 to $57.54 this week, while all non-zero credit prices decreased by 2.25% WoW, falling from $70.14 to $68.56. Type 1 trades saw 177,849 credits exchanged, reflecting a fall of 84.20% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a decrease of 35.20% to reach 1,098,904 credits.Â 

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_593609" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 5.24% WoW, reaching $56.38/MT. On 11th March, the average spot price was $57.75/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $63.00/MT.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_593610" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 46 trades were reported in the market, with five high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $85.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 15,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $50.00 per credit of Type 2, reported for a transaction at volume of 10,000 credits. The smallest volume transfers of 40 credits of the Type 1 category were undertaken for a price of $72.50. The largest volume transfers of 500,000 credits of the Type 2 category were undertaken for a price of $72.75.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_593611" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] In February, following the OAL's disapproval of the LCFS amendments, the market saw significant changes. The average Type 1 spot price was $72.15, with total trading volumes reaching 6.31 MMT for the month. This reflects an 18.22% year-over-year increase from February 2024, when the average spot price was $61.03 and volumes were 1.96 MMT. In the first week of March, the Type 1 average spot price declined to $57.54, falling below February levels.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_593612" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 03rd March â€“ 09th March, total VPT recorded a 5.70% decrease (WoW), with all non-zero prices falling over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices decreased by 0.80% WoW, D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices decreased by 5.19% WoW each. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • EPA Announces E15 Implementation Deadline Extensions for South Dakota, Ohio. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) announced its intent to act on requests from South Dakota and Ohio seeking a one-year extension of the current implementation date to remove the 1-psi volatility waiver for E10. This action follows the February 26, 2025 deadline for states to submit written requests for delay. Kansas, which was not among the eight states addressed in the final rule, submitted a similar request.
  • S. Congressman Max Miller and Colleagues Reintroduce the Farm to Fly Act to Lift American Farmers to New Heights. U.S. Congressman Max Miller (OH-07) joined by a bipartisan group of representatives reintroduced the Farm to Fly Act to create new, robust markets for American agricultural products and strengthen domestic energy resources on 28 Feb 2025. Senators Moran (R-KS), Grassley (R-IA), Duckworth (D-IL), Ricketts (R-NE), Klochubar (D-MN), and Ernst (R-IA) introduced the Senate companion, S.144, in January. The Farm to Fly Act was largely adopted within the 2024 House Agriculture Committee Farm Bill. The bill would foster the development of Sustainable Aviation Fuels (SAF) within existing U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) programs, allow for greater collaboration, and ensure USDAâ€™s SAF definitions reflect eligibility for American agricultural crops.
  • Extension of 2024 Renewable Fuel Standard Compliance Deadline â€“ Final Rulemaking. EPA announced a final rule to extend the Renewable Fuel Standard compliance reporting deadline for the 2024 compliance year. EPA is also making several minor amendments and technical corrections to other RFS provisions.
  • Clean Fuels, Trade Groups Applaud Bipartisan, Bicameral Renewable Fuel for Ocean-Going Vessels Act. Clean Fuels Alliance America and other groups representing agriculture and maritime fleets thanked Senate and House champions for introducing the bipartisan Renewable Fuel for Ocean-Going Vessels Act. Sens. Pete Ricketts (R-NE) and Amy Klobuchar (D-MN) sponsored the legislation in the Senate. Reps. Mariannette Miller-Meeks (R-IA), John Garamendi (D-CA) and six others sponsored identical legislation in the House of Representatives.
  • Clean Fuels Expresses Disappointment in RFS Delays. Clean Fuels Alliance America expressed frustration over ongoing delays in the Renewable Fuel Standard program. EPA this evening finalized a rule to delay compliance with the 2024 RFS standards; the compliance deadline would have fallen on March 31.
